QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_64

question.text: What is 'delivery margin' in the context of physical settlement of futures contracts?

answer.text: Additional margin charged on physically-settled futures contracts close to expiry to ensure both parties can fulfill delivery obligations — it is imposed during the delivery period on top of regular margins
The margin refunded when a futures contract is settled by delivery
The standard initial margin that applies throughout the life of the contract
A margin specific to digital delivery of contract notes

explanation.text: **The Logic:** As physically-settled contracts approach expiry, the exchange imposes delivery margin (higher than normal) to ensure the long has funds for delivery and the short has securities. This typically starts 4-5 days before expiry and increases progressively. It applies to stock F&O and commodity futures with physical settlement. **The Trap:** Delivery margin is ADDITIONAL, not the regular margin. It's not refunded — it's a temporary increased margin requirement. It only applies to physically-settled contracts. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch7 covers delivery margins for stock F&O. Series XVI Ch7 covers commodity delivery margins. Cash-settled contracts (index futures, currency futures) don't have delivery margins. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Delivery margin increases can significantly raise capital requirements near expiry — plan to close or roll positions well before the delivery margin kicks in unless you intend to take/give delivery.
